title: Canary rollout latency regression in prod (no auto-mitigation); request SLO-based auto-rollback for model rollouts
preview:
Issue summary
Northstar HR reported elevated tail latency on their Dedicated endpoint during a model canary rollout. The customer observed p95 and p99 latency increases and intermittent client timeouts during peak traffic. The rollout did not automatically pause/rollback, requiring manual intervention by on-call.

Customer impact
- Customer-facing latency degradation for chat completions.
- Intermittent client-side timeouts (HTTP 499s observed from the gateway; no increase in 5xx at the platform edge initially).
- The customer explicitly requested an automated safety rail: SLO-based auto-rollback (or auto-pause) for canary/A-B rollouts.

Environment
- Dedicated deployment
- Regions: us-east (primary), eu-west (secondary)
- Route: /v1/chat/completions (tenant: northstar-hr)
- Rollout type: canary traffic shift (10% -> 25% planned)

Timeline (all times PT)
- 2026-02-17 09:42: Rollout step increased candidate traffic from 10% to 25% in us-east.
- 09:49: First internal alert observed on rollout dashboard: p99 latency +38% vs baseline (us-east) for candidate.
- 09:52: Customer email to TAM (Liam) + support@ noting “timeouts + slow responses”.
- 09:57: #incidents thread opened; SRE begins triage.
- 10:05: Rollout manually paused at 25% in us-east; traffic held.
- 10:11: Manual rollback executed to baseline model version; traffic shifted back to 100% baseline.
- 10:18: Latency returns to normal ranges; customer confirms improvement.

What happened / initial findings
- Regression appears isolated to candidate model build/config in us-east under real traffic mix.
- Affected metrics were primarily tail latency (p99) with some p95 increase; error-rate SLO did not breach, and the existing monitor was not rollout-scoped (could not attribute to candidate vs baseline quickly).
- We did not have auto-mitigation wired: rollout controller did not evaluate burn-rate windows against rollout-scoped SLOs, so no automatic pause/rollback occurred.

Customer request
Please provide:
1) Auto-pause/auto-rollback when latency or error-rate SLOs breach during a rollout.
2) Ability to configure thresholds and windows per route/model/region/tier.
3) Audit trail explaining why a rollback happened (metrics snapshot + policy used).

Links
- Slack incident thread: sources/slack/incidents/1770705405.json
- Additional on-call notes on monitor staleness/rollout scoping: sources/slack/eng-sre/1770246692.json
- Internal postmortem/notes: sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/postmortems/2026-02-17-rollout-latency-regression-auto-rollback-gaps.json

Requested next steps (support-facing)
- Provide customer with a clear explanation of current rollout safety behavior and near-term mitigation.
- Share that SLO-driven auto-rollback is in-flight (project: Auto-rollback on SLO breach integration) and offer to include Northstar HR in early access once available.

Workaround (current)
- Keep candidate traffic capped at <=10% for this model family in us-east until regression root cause is addressed.
- Require manual monitoring + explicit SRE sign-off for step increases during business hours.

Resolution (support ticket)
Manual rollback performed; performance stabilized. Follow-up actions tracked in Eng/PM systems for SLO-scoped monitors and auto-rollback integration.

title: Audit log pipeline alerts too noisy after rollout (drop-rate/latency) — tune thresholds + update runbook guidance
preview:
Issue Summary
- Since enabling the initial audit log delivery alerts (latency + drop rate), On-Call has been paged repeatedly for short-lived, low-impact fluctuations. The alerts are generating noise and are not aligned with the intended incident thresholds for audit pipeline reliability.

Impact
- Increased on-call load and alert fatigue for #eng-oncall / #eng-sre.
- Several pages triggered during expected/benign conditions (brief exporter backpressure, transient ingestion backlog recoveries, low-volume tenants).
- Risk: real incidents may get ignored or delayed due to repeated false positives.

What we’re seeing
- Drop-rate alert firing during short ingestion backlogs or during exporter retries, even when events are later delivered (at-least-once semantics).
- Latency alert firing on p95 spikes during low traffic periods, especially in regions with fewer events.
- Alerts firing per-dimension (region/service) without sufficient volume gating.

Scope
- Tune thresholds and evaluation windows for audit log pipeline alerts.
- Add volume gating / minimum sample requirements.
- Update the audit pipeline incident runbook to reflect new alert semantics and include a ‘noise triage’ checklist.

Notes / Context
- The audit log delivery SLO and dashboards were recently rolled out; the current alerting configuration appears overly sensitive.
- This ticket is intended to adjust alerting behavior without changing core pipeline delivery semantics.

Proposed changes (implementation guidance)
1) Drop-rate alert
- Change evaluation from short-window percentage to burn-rate style alerting with multiple windows (fast + slow) to reduce flapping.
- Add minimum-event-volume gating (do not page if below N events in window).
- Keep a lower-severity warning notification for early signals (non-paging).

2) Latency alert
- Page only on sustained p95 delivery latency above threshold for >= 15m (instead of 5m), plus volume gating.
- Prefer region-aggregated SLO for paging; keep per-region/per-service as non-paging signals.

3) Exporter queue/backpressure
- Convert “queue depth” alerts to warnings unless backlog grows monotonically for >= 20m.
- Ensure dashboards show “ingestion backlog vs exporter backlog” to route the incident correctly.

Acceptance Criteria
- Paging alerts align with actionable incidents (sustained drop-rate or sustained high delivery latency).
- On-call pages reduce materially (target: <2 pages/week for audit pipeline absent real incident).
- Runbook updated with new thresholds, triage steps, and links to the relevant dashboards.

Owner / Reviewers
- Owner: Sean Gallagher (SRE)
- Consult: Rafael Mendes (runbook), Aisha Bello (alerting/dashboards), Logan Wright (coverage telemetry impacts)

title: Quality gate runs timing out in eu-west (Optimize gating) causing config changes to remain blocked
preview:
Issue summary:
Northstar reports Optimize config changes in eu-west are getting stuck in "Gate running" and then failing with a timeout. Because gating is required for apply, changes remain blocked.

Customer impact:
- Optimize recommendations cannot be applied for eu-west routes (both one-click and auto-apply policies).
- Customer is unable to progress planned cost reduction work; sees repeated "Gate run timed out" failures.
- No production traffic regression reported; this is a control-plane / eval execution reliability issue.

Environment:
- Hosted API workloads
- Region: eu-west
- Routes: northstar-prod-search (primary), northstar-prod-support-chat (secondary)

What the customer sees:
- Console shows Gate status: Running → Failed (Timeout)
- Gate run duration reported ~45-60 min before failing

Steps to reproduce (support repro):
1) In Console → Optimize → Recommendations, select a recommendation that changes batching policy ("increase max_batch_tokens" / enable continuous batching profile).
2) Click "Run quality gates".
3) Gate transitions to Running.
4) After extended period, gate fails with "Timed out waiting for eval-harness completion".

Observed error text (Console):
"GateRunFailed: timed_out. The eval run did not complete within the configured deadline. gate_run_id=gr_01JMBE8E2Q0K5G9H7X2M4KJ9P1"

Triage notes:
Initial suspicion is eu-west GPU job queue delays in eval-harness (capacity constraints) rather than test flakiness (no metrics returned; job never completes).

Artifacts / identifiers:
- Example gate_run_id: gr_01JMBE8E2Q0K5G9H7X2M4KJ9P1
- Example eval_run_id (from Optimize logs): eh_01JMBE9M7W3F0QJ7D3Y2VQ0YQD
- Correlation IDs: rw_req_2f3c1b2d3b1a, rw_req_89a9c0d7e11f

Internal pointers:
- Slack: #support thread started by PM (Aditya) with customer screenshots; #eng-sre pinged for eu-west capacity.
- Runbook followed: runbook-optimize-quality-gate-failures (timeout path).

title: Latency regression (p95) on model-xyz after runtime release (us-east Dedicated)
preview:
Issue summary
- Customer (Tensorlytics) reports increased p95 latency on Dedicated traffic for model-xyz immediately following Redwood runtime release rollout in us-east.
- Report references internal canary alert firing around the same time.

Customer impact
- Elevated p95 end-to-end latency on generation requests; intermittent client-side timeouts (customer timeout set to 3.0s).
- Customer reports downstream job SLA impact on their real-time pipeline.

Scope / environment
- Environment: prod / us-east / Dedicated (Tensorlytics)
- Model: model-xyz (customer pinned; no model-version change reported by customer)
- Start time (customer-reported): ~2026-02-07 08:40 UTC
- Runtime change window: runtime rollout started ~2026-02-07 08:15 UTC (us-east dedicated pools)

Observed metrics (Support + SRE verification)
- Baseline (prior 24h): p95 e2e latency ~1.25s; tokens/sec steady (within normal variance)
- During incident window: p95 e2e latency ~1.85–2.10s (+45–65%); tokens/sec down ~15–20%
- perf-canary signal (internal): warning->paging transition for model-xyz p95 latency regression in us-east, sustained >30m

Initial hypothesis
- Regression is likely runtime/kernel-level (execution time increase) vs scheduler queueing, based on early perf-canary breakdown showing runtime-only latency shift with queueing roughly flat.

Steps to reproduce (internal)
1) Target us-east Dedicated inference endpoint for Tensorlytics pool.
2) Run synthetic prompts in the canary “medium+long prefill” buckets (prompt lengths ~1.5k–3k tokens; typical decode 128–256).
3) Compare runtime-only prefill and decode latency vs baseline artifact for model-xyz + H100-SXM class.

Triage notes / correlation
- Canary alert thread indicated a kernel tag change concurrent with runtime build change (kernel selection identifier changed for the prefill path).
- Queueing-time metrics did not show a corresponding increase; batch size distribution showed a mild downward shift but not enough to explain the full p95 movement.

Mitigation applied
- Rolled back runtime build for the affected dedicated pool in us-east to previous known-good build for model-xyz traffic.
- Additionally pinned kernel selection to prior kernel set for model-xyz (temporary safety measure while investigating new kernel path).

Outcome
- Post-rollback p95 returned to within ~5–10% of baseline within ~20 minutes; tokens/sec recovered.
- Customer confirmed latency returned to normal in their app monitoring.

Follow-ups
- Runtime team to root-cause new kernel path regression (prefill-heavy bucket) and add guardrail in kernel selection for model-xyz until fixed.
- SRE/Release Eng to update rollout checklist: require “top model” perf-canary green for 60 minutes before expanding runtime rollout in Dedicated.
- Support: send customer incident summary + confirmation of rollback, and note we will provide an RCA once available.

Customer-facing note (approved wording)
- “We identified a performance regression in our serving runtime rollout affecting model-xyz in us-east Dedicated. We mitigated by rolling back the runtime to the previous build, and latency has returned to normal. We’re continuing root-cause analysis on the new runtime path and will share more details and preventive steps when complete.”

title: Demo tenant hitting 429 throttling during live customer demo (Northstar Payments)
preview:
Issue summary
- During a live SE-led demo for Northstar Payments, the Redwood demo tenant began returning HTTP 429 responses mid-flow (streaming chat + tool call step). This caused the demo to stall and required switching to backup screenshots for the Console section.

Customer impact
- Live demo interruption (~6–8 minutes). Customer saw repeated 429s and intermittent retries.
- Risk: undermines reliability story and raises concern about quota predictability/noisy-neighbor.

When / where
- Date/time: 2025-11-12 ~19:10–19:25 UTC
- Environment: prod / us-east
- Tenant: Redwood internal demo org ("demo tenant")

Observed behavior
- Chat/completions requests started returning:
  - 429 Too Many Requests
  - Response headers included rate-limit remaining=0 (per-minute)
- Retries from demo app increased request volume, exacerbating throttling.

Expected behavior
- Demo tenant should be sized for at least 2 parallel demos, with headroom for retries/streaming and synthetic traffic seeding.

Steps to reproduce (as seen during call)
1) Start demo app scenario runner (streaming chat)
2) Execute tool-calling step (adds 1–2 additional calls)
3) Open Console in parallel (refreshes usage/cost panels)
4) Within ~2 minutes, observe 429s

Initial hypotheses
- Demo API key configured with self-serve default RPM/TPM limits.
- Synthetic traffic generator + live demo sharing same project/key.
- Parallel demos earlier in the hour consumed burst capacity; rate-limit window not reset.

Investigation notes
- Checked gateway logs for the demo org/project: throttles triggered on request-rate limit (RPM), not GPU capacity.
- No elevated 5xx in serving runtime; this was an API gateway quota/rate-limit enforcement event.
- Console page refreshes were not the root cause but contributed to request count and distracted during recovery.

Mitigation applied (during incident)
- Temporary quota uplift applied to demo org/project in us-east:
  - Increased RPM cap + burst allowance
  - Increased TPM cap to reduce retry amplification for streaming
- Confirmed new limits took effect within ~60 seconds; 429s stopped.

Root cause
- Demo tenant was provisioned with quota defaults intended for standard self-serve tenants, and our automation did not reliably apply the higher demo profile. Additionally, synthetic traffic seeding was configured to use the same project/key as live demos, consuming part of the minute-based request budget.

Resolution
- Short-term: raise demo tenant quotas and move synthetic traffic generator to a dedicated "seed" project/key to isolate from live demo activity.
- Long-term: update demo tenant provisioning automation defaults to always apply demo quota profile + create separate scoped keys (live-demo vs seed-traffic), and add a demo healthcheck gate that verifies quota/rate-limit settings before SEs start a call.

Customer-facing notes (what SE said)
- Framed as: "We hit our safety rate limit in the demo environment; production customers set limits appropriate to their workloads, and Dedicated capacity has separate controls. We’ll adjust the demo tenant so it never happens again."

Follow-ups / action items
- [DONE] Document immediate workaround in demo runbook: if 429s occur, switch to backup mode and run healthcheck.
- [DONE] Add healthcheck validation for quota/rate limits and surface clear pass/fail output (linked PR).
- [DONE] Provisioning automation: ensure demo profile quotas applied idempotently; prevent seed job from sharing live-demo key.
- [TODO] Add alerting on demo-tenant approaching RPM/TPM caps (warning threshold) routed to demo on-call alias.

Workarounds (SE playbook)
- Run demo tenant healthcheck endpoint; if quotas not applied, page #support and switch to backup screenshots.
- If throttling persists, pause synthetic traffic generator for the live window and retry after 60s rate-limit window reset.

Notes on data/security
- All traffic is synthetic/demo; no customer data involved.

References
- Slack: #support and #sales thread (internal) around live demo throttling
- Confluence: Demo capacity/quotas runbook + demo tenant reset runbook
- Related: SUP-4189 (Console dashboards empty; seed traffic generator separation work overlaps)

title: Latency regression after runtime-1.22 canary: elevated p99 decode latency correlated with KV compaction activity
preview:
Issue Summary
- During a runtime-1.22 canary expansion in prod (hosted, us-east-1), we observed a statistically significant p99 latency regression on decode-heavy traffic, most visible for mixed sequence-length distributions.
- Regression correlated with elevated KV compaction work (bytes moved + compaction time) and increased allocator fragmentation ratio oscillation.
- Canary was rolled back (feature flag disabled + cohort drained) per guardrails.

Customer Impact
- Customer: Northwind Analytics (enterprise) reported increased p99 latency and intermittent request timeouts during bursty chat traffic.
- Symptoms: p95 largely stable; p99 degraded (notable tail spikes) during bursts and after churn events (rapid session start/stop).
- Scope: Only canary cohort in us-east-1. Control region (eu-west-1) did not show comparable change.

Detection
- 2026-01-29 09:12 PT: SRE alert fired: hosted decode p99 exceeded threshold for canary cohort for 10m.
- 2026-01-29 09:18 PT: Support received escalation via CSM thread referencing prior tail-latency concerns (see SUP-28411).

Environment
- Runtime: runtime-1.22 canary (hosted)
- Canary cohort: ~5% of us-east-1 hosted capacity (dedicated pools not included)
- Traffic pattern: mixed prompt lengths, bursty chat sessions, high cancellation/churn

Evidence / Observations
- Dashboard signals (high level):
  - p99 decode latency: +18–35% vs baseline during burst windows
  - Compaction bytes moved: spiky (peaks aligned with p99 spikes)
  - Compaction time per step: higher variance than staging
  - Eviction counters: low/near-zero (so tail impact likely compaction rather than eviction)
  - Fragmentation ratio: saw sawtooth behavior (trigger threshold repeatedly crossed)
- Trace exemplars (from compaction spans): decode loop shows intermittent stalls where compaction step executed near decode boundary.

Timeline / Comments
- 2026-01-29 09:22 PT (Sean Gallagher, SRE): Confirmed alert corresponds to canary nodes only. Requesting runtime team to validate compaction budget enforcement and whether compaction can run during decode.
- 2026-01-29 09:31 PT (Rafael Mendes, Oncall): Opened incident bridge in #incidents; linked thread: sources/slack/incidents/1767735109.json. Requested latest canary diff + feature flag defaults.
- 2026-01-29 09:40 PT (Sofia Ivanova, Runtime): Suspected scheduler boundary issue: compaction budget being applied but step can still run in an unfavorable phase when queues are shallow (burst recovery) and decode becomes latency-sensitive.
- 2026-01-29 09:55 PT (Allison Grant, Observability): Noted instrumentation overhead increase in the same canary build (compaction tracing spans). Suggested testing with spans disabled to isolate.
- 2026-01-29 10:05 PT (Decision): Roll back canary per hosted tail-latency guardrails. Disable KV compaction feature flag in hosted us-east-1 canary cohort; drain cohort to baseline build.
- 2026-01-29 10:11 PT (Rafael Mendes): Executed kill-switch via config propagation (validated propagation <2m). p99 began recovering within 15m as cohort drained.
- 2026-01-29 10:38 PT (Support): Informed Northwind Analytics we rolled back the canary change and are investigating; requested a 30-minute window of request IDs for trace correlation.
- 2026-01-29 12:10 PT (Samir Qureshi, Runtime): Preliminary hypothesis: compaction relocation step sometimes exceeds time budget due to larger-than-expected copy size when fragmentation is high; budget accounting is per-step but actual move cost depends on page locality. Also flagged potential overhead from tracing spans.
- 2026-01-30 08:45 PT (Runtime): Added follow-up to test: disable compaction spans (keep metrics) and re-run canary in a smaller cohort; additionally adjust compaction trigger hysteresis to reduce repeated threshold crossings.
- 2026-02-03 16:20 PT (Rafael Mendes): Confirmed no further p99 regressions after rollback; customer reports normalized behavior. Closing support incident; tracking engineering fixes under runtime epic/tickets.

Rollback / Mitigation Actions Taken
- Rolled back canary cohort in us-east-1 hosted (KV compaction kill-switch OFF).
- Left fragmentation metrics ON for continued monitoring.
- Paused further canary expansion pending fixes + perf canary pass.

Root Cause (Provisional)
- KV compaction work occasionally executed at an unfavorable time relative to decode scheduling, causing transient stalls that manifest as p99 spikes under bursty mixed-length workloads.
- Contributing factors:
  - Compaction trigger oscillation (threshold crossed repeatedly) causing repeated compaction attempts.
  - Increased tracing span overhead in the same build (amplifying tail effects).

Resolution
- Customer-facing: Regression resolved by rolling back canary (feature flag disabled; nodes drained to baseline).
- Internal: Incident closed with action items assigned; canary expansion blocked until updated perf canary scenario and revised compaction budgeting/hysteresis are validated.

Follow-up Tasks / Next Steps
- Runtime (owner: Sofia Ivanova / Samir Qureshi):
  - Tighten scheduler phase gating: ensure compaction steps never run on latency-critical decode path when queue depth is below threshold.
  - Add hysteresis + cooldown to fragmentation trigger to prevent sawtooth compaction behavior.
  - Verify budget enforcement uses wall-clock + bytes moved with hard stop when approaching decode deadlines.
- Observability (owner: Allison Grant):
  - Add dashboard panel overlay: p99 vs compaction bytes moved and compaction step time.
  - Reduce tracing overhead or sample compaction spans; keep metrics at full fidelity.
- SRE (owner: Sean Gallagher):
  - Update automated rollback condition to consider compaction bytes moved rate + p99 correlation (not just p99 absolute).

External Communication
- Northwind Analytics updated 2026-01-29: rollback performed; performance back to baseline; follow-up planned after next canary with improved guardrails.

References
- Slack incident thread: sources/slack/incidents/1767735109.json
- Oncall procedures draft: sources/slack/eng-oncall/1767660044.json
- Guardrails: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/hosted-api-tail-latency-guardrails.json
- Runbook: sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-kv-cache-compaction-runbook.json

title: Latency regression suspected after runtime upgrade (p99 TTFT + p99 end-to-end) on dedicated deployment
preview:
Issue summary
- Customer reports a noticeable tail-latency regression after a runtime upgrade on their Dedicated deployment.
- Correlates with internal Q1 baseline sweep signals: a similar p99 TTFT regression signature appeared in perf-canary for long-prefill + mixed chat workloads, but was initially classified as canary-only/no-customer-impact until this report.

Customer impact
- HelioBank reports increased time-to-first-token (TTFT) and overall response time on streaming chat endpoints.
- Impact: degraded UX (customer-facing assistant), increased client-side timeouts, and higher retry volume.
- Customer states this started “within the last ~24 hours” following their scheduled runtime upgrade window.

Timeline (customer-provided)
- 2026-01-18 21:00–23:00 UTC: Dedicated runtime upgrade applied (customer acknowledged maintenance window).
- 2026-01-19 07:30 UTC: First internal alert from customer-side synthetic checks.
- 2026-01-19 15:10 UTC: Customer opens support ticket with request IDs and metrics screenshots.

Environment
- Deployment: Dedicated
- Regions: primary us-east; spillover / DR traffic in eu-west
- Endpoint: /v1/chat/completions (stream=true)
- Runtime build reported by customer: redwood-runtime 2026.01.18.1 (from response headers)
- Previous runtime build (known good): redwood-runtime 2026.01.11.3

Observed symptoms (initial)
- p99 TTFT up ~35–55% on llama-3.1-70b-instruct for peak hours in us-east.
- p95 TTFT up ~15–25%.
- Tokens/sec steady or slightly down (~0–8%), suggesting the regression is dominated by queueing/prefill scheduling and/or kernel selection rather than decode throughput.
- Higher variance than typical; customer notes “spiky” p99.

What we checked (internal)
- Baseline dashboards: p99 TTFT regression for long-prefill cohort in us-east on the same runtime line (matches customer window). Similar but smaller effect in eu-west.
- Scheduler telemetry: increased queue_wait_ms and batch_assembly_ms during the impacted period; batch size distribution shifted toward smaller batches under burst.
- Kernel selection: preliminary suspicion of unintended fallback on one sequence-length regime (requires kernel debug dump to confirm).

Hypotheses
1) Scheduler backpressure thresholds interacting with customer’s traffic shape (bursty tool-calling + occasional long-prefill) causing higher queue wait for chat requests.
2) Kernel auto-selection fallback triggered for certain context lengths, increasing prefill latency and contributing to TTFT.
3) Fleet-level variance: GPU clocking / placement differences post-upgrade (less likely given correlation across canary + customer).

Requested from customer
- 10–20 request IDs spanning before/after (from their client logs).
- Confirmation whether caching mode changed (cold vs warm); customer states “no explicit caching config changes,” but we will validate server-side.

Mitigation plan (proposed)
- Immediate: pin/roll back runtime to 2026.01.11.3 for HelioBank Dedicated cluster if regression confirmed (fastest path).
- Short-term: apply scheduler config override for long-prefill backpressure guardrails if rollback is not possible.
- Follow-up: patch-forward with validated fix once root cause is confirmed, and add this traffic shape to the representative suite gating thresholds.

Dashboards / links (internal)
- Grafana: Perf Baseline / TTFT by cohort (us-east): https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/perf-baselines/ttft?var_region=us-east&var_customer=heliobank
- Grafana: Dedicated cluster saturation + queueing: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/dedicated-runtime/queueing?var_cluster=ded-heliobank-us-east
- Looker: Customer latency SLO view (streaming): https://looker.redwood.internal/explore/slo_latency?customer=HelioBank
- Logs (sample): splunk://search?query=customer=HelioBank%20ttft_ms%20model=llama-3.1-70b-instruct%20earliest=-48h

Support ask
- Please confirm whether Redwood can revert/pin the runtime version for HelioBank’s Dedicated cluster and provide an ETA. Customer is requesting mitigation within 24 hours.

title: Console: Rollback button not visible for on-call operator (RBAC permission denied / role mapping misconfigured)
preview:
Issue summary
- On-call operator cannot see the "Rollback" button in the Console for Optimization Config versions.
- This blocks rollback during gameday / incident response and is confusing because the operator is in the expected on-call group.

Impact
- Internal: on-call cannot execute one-click rollback via Console (must escalate to Platform team or use break-glass).
- Risk: delayed mitigation if a bad optimization config causes latency/5xx regression.

Who is affected
- Internal users in Okta/SSO group: sre-oncall (expected to have Optimization Change Operator privileges).

Observed behavior
- UI: No "Rollback" CTA shown on version detail page.
- API: direct call to rollback endpoint returns 403.

Expected behavior
- Members of the on-call operator group should have permission to rollback optimization configs within approved scopes.

Error details
- Console network call response:
  - POST /v1/optimization-configs/rollouts/{rollout_id}:rollback
  - HTTP 403
  - Body: {"error":"permission_denied","message":"missing permission: optimization_config.rollback"}

Notes
- This started after the RBAC policy bundle update earlier this week (Console safe-apply/rollback internal beta enablement).

title: Grant least-privilege permissions for perf-canary service account (inference read + metrics write)
preview:
Request type: IAM / service account permissions

Requester: Noah Patel (Serving Runtime)
Service: perf-canary (prod)
Service account: svc-perf-canary@prod.redwood-inference.iam (created via terraform; see pr-18712)
Target start date: Week of 2026-01-19 (phase-1 rollout prep)

Background:
We’re rolling out the always-on perf-canary that sends synthetic requests to internal inference endpoints and emits per-model performance metrics (latency, tokens/sec, queueing breakdown). Canary does not use customer prompts; only synthetic prompt shapes.

Needed permissions (least privilege):
1) Inference read/invoke:
- Ability for svc-perf-canary to call internal inference endpoints in prod for a curated allowlist of models.
- Auth scope expected: "canary:invoke" (added in pr-19197). Please ensure the service account is bound to this scope in prod.
- Network path: via internal API gateway (same as other internal services). No console access needed.

2) Metrics write:
- Ability to emit metrics to telemetry ingestion (Prometheus remote_write / metrics gateway used by services).
- Expected permission/role: telemetry.metrics:write (or equivalent internal role used for service metrics emission).
- No log ingestion or trace write required for MVP (metrics only).

Non-goals / explicitly NOT requested:
- No ability to read customer prompts, request payload logs, or production data stores.
- No admin access to model registry, control plane, or runtime fleets.

Acceptance criteria:
- perf-canary can successfully call the internal inference endpoint in prod using svc-perf-canary credentials and receives 200 responses.
- perf-canary can emit metrics without 403/permission errors (confirmed by metrics appearing in Grafana/Prometheus within ~5–10 minutes).

Operational notes:
- Canary QPS is capped; overhead budget <0.5% GPU capacity per region.
- We will validate in a single region (us-east) with 2–3 models before enabling paging.

Point of contact for validation:
- Noah Patel (runtime perf canary TL)
- Nadia Rahman (telemetry pipeline)

Please advise if you need the exact endpoint hostname and/or metrics remote_write destination; we can provide in a secure channel.

title: Benchmark fleet service account missing IAM/RBAC permissions (GPU jobs + artifact export)
preview:
Issue summary
- The benchmark fleet runner service account cannot (a) create/run GPU benchmark jobs reliably and (b) export run artifacts (manifests, traces, JSON results) to the shared artifacts bucket.
- This started after the benchmark fleet moved to Workload Identity + a new namespace split for perf-canary vs ad-hoc runs.

Impact
- Blocks Q1 2026 perf baseline sweeps and perf-canary nightly runs from producing publishable artifacts.
- Engineers can sometimes run locally, but CI and scheduled runs fail, leading to missing data in dashboards and delayed regression triage.

Who is affected
- Serving Runtime benchmarking owners + SRE perf-canary pipeline.

Environment
- Benchmark fleet Kubernetes clusters: bench-us-east (primary), bench-eu-west (secondary)
- Namespace(s): benchmark-lab, perf-canary
- Runner identity:
  - KSA: perf-runner (namespace: perf-canary)
  - KSA: benchmark-runner (namespace: benchmark-lab)
  - GSA (expected): bench-fleet-runner@redwood-inference.iam.gserviceaccount.com
- Artifact destinations:
  - GCS: gs://rw-benchmark-artifacts-us-east1/perf-suite/
  - GCS: gs://rw-benchmark-artifacts-eu-west1/perf-suite/
  - (optional) BigQuery dataset used by dashboard ingest: rw_perf_bench

Symptoms / errors
1) GPU jobs fail at image pull (intermittent but common)
- Pod events:
  - Failed to pull image "us-docker.pkg.dev/redwood-prod/benchmark-lab/runner:2026.01.14": rpc error: code = Unknown desc = failed to authorize: failed to fetch anonymous token: unexpected status from GET request to https://us-docker.pkg.dev/v2/token: 403 Forbidden
  - ImagePullBackOff

2) Artifact export fails at the end of runs
- Export step logs:
  - gsutil cp results.json gs://rw-benchmark-artifacts-us-east1/perf-suite/INT-8821-test/...
  - AccessDeniedException: 403 bench-fleet-runner@redwood-inference.iam.gserviceaccount.com does not have storage.objects.create access to the Google Cloud Storage object.

3) Some runs create pods but fail to attach GPU resources in perf-canary namespace
- Kubernetes error surfaced by harness:
  - Error from server (Forbidden): pods is forbidden: User "system:serviceaccount:perf-canary:perf-runner" cannot create resource "pods" in API group "" in the namespace "perf-canary"

Steps to reproduce
1) From CI (perf-canary) trigger the representative suite nightly job.
2) Observe failure at either:
  - Pod creation in perf-canary namespace (RBAC Forbidden), or
  - Image pull from Artifact Registry (403), or
  - Result upload to GCS bucket (403).

Investigation notes (internal)
- Confirmed failures on both us-east and eu-west benchmark clusters.
- K8s service accounts exist but are not consistently bound to the expected Google service account.
- RBAC in perf-canary namespace diverged from benchmark-lab after namespace split; benchmark-lab had RoleBinding granting create/list/watch pods/jobs, perf-canary did not.
- Artifact Registry access appears to require explicit reader permission on the repo; previously inherited via a broader project-level role that was removed during least-privilege cleanup.
- GCS bucket permissions were granted to the old GSA (bench-fleet@...), not the new bench-fleet-runner@... account.

Root cause
A) Kubernetes RBAC missing in perf-canary namespace
- The perf-canary namespace was created without the Role/RoleBinding that grants the runner KSA permissions to create Jobs/Pods and read Pod status/events.

B) Workload Identity binding incomplete for one KSA
- The KSA (perf-canary/perf-runner) was not annotated with iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account=bench-fleet-runner@... in one cluster, causing requests to use the node default identity (no access).

C) IAM regressions during least-privilege cleanup
- bench-fleet-runner@... lacked:
  - Artifact Registry read access to the benchmark-lab runner repository
  - GCS object create permissions on rw-benchmark-artifacts-* buckets
  - Logging writer permissions for structured benchmark logs

Resolution
1) Fixed K8s RBAC (both clusters)
- Added Role + RoleBinding in perf-canary namespace to allow the runner to:
  - create/get/list/watch pods
  - create/get/list/watch batch/jobs
  - get/list/watch pods/log and events

2) Fixed Workload Identity binding
- Annotated KSAs:
  - perf-canary/perf-runner
  - benchmark-lab/benchmark-runner
  with the intended GSA bench-fleet-runner@redwood-inference.iam.gserviceaccount.com
- Added iam.workloadIdentityUser binding to allow those KSAs to impersonate the GSA.

3) Restored required GCP IAM roles to bench-fleet-runner@...
- Artifact Registry:
  - Granted roles/artifactregistry.reader on repo: redwood-prod/benchmark-lab
- GCS buckets:
  - Granted roles/storage.objectAdmin on:
    - rw-benchmark-artifacts-us-east1 (restricted to perf-suite/* prefix via bucket policy where supported)
    - rw-benchmark-artifacts-eu-west1 (restricted similarly)
- Observability:
  - Granted roles/logging.logWriter (to ensure harness logs are not dropped)
- Dashboard ingest (optional path used by nightly):
  - Granted minimal BigQuery dataset write role to rw_perf_bench ingest service (not to the runner) and updated pipeline to use ingest SA instead of runner SA (reduces blast radius).

Verification
- Ran a smoke benchmark in perf-canary namespace on us-east:
  - Pod scheduled with GPU, image pulled successfully, job completed.
  - Artifact upload succeeded; results.json + manifest.json present under gs://rw-benchmark-artifacts-us-east1/perf-suite/smoke/2026-01-16/
- Re-ran on eu-west:
  - Same outcome; no 403s.
- Confirmed perf-canary nightly run unblocked (next scheduled run expected 2026-01-16 03:00 UTC).

Follow-ups / notes
- Please keep IAM changes in Terraform (single source of truth). Manual console grants were avoided; any emergency grants done during triage were removed after Terraform apply.
- Recommend adding a startup check in the harness to assert:
  - can create Job in target namespace
  - can read/write to artifact bucket
  - can pull runner image
  and fail fast with actionable error.

Requester notes
- Rina: needs this resolved before the next baseline sweep window reservation (Q1 2026 baseline plan).

title: Orbit Analytics: p95 latency spike after runtime config update in Dedicated H100 pool (suspected hardware tuning profile rollout)
preview:
Issue summary:
- Customer reports a sudden increase in tail latency (p95/p99) beginning ~2025-12-08 09:40 PT on their Dedicated H100 pool (orbit-h100-prod, us-east).
- No customer-side deploy at that time. Customer correlates onset with Redwood runtime config update/rollout.

- Primary impact: p95 end-to-end latency for chat completions increased from ~650–800ms to ~1.3–1.6s for their typical workloads.

- Suspected: H100 hardware tuning profile pack enabled (scheduler preset + kernel heuristics) in orbit-h100-prod pool.

- Dedicated: orbit-h100-prod
- Region: us-east
- GPUs: H100 (mixed SXM/PCIe per fleet notes; exact mix unknown at time of report)
- Model: llama-3.1-70b-instruct pinned
- Traffic: mixture of streaming and non-streaming; concurrency bursts at top of minute (customer cron-driven workloads)

Requested by customer:
- Immediate rollback/disable of new profile if confirmed.
- Evidence: graphs before/after + explanation of expected behavior/trade-offs.

Attachments/logs requested:
- Customer request IDs and timestamps around spike (pending at ticket creation).

title: Demo tenant: SSE streaming disconnects intermittently in eu-west during live demos
preview:
Issue summary:
During live demos using the demo tenant in eu-west, Server-Sent Events (SSE) streaming for chat completions intermittently disconnects mid-response. Customers see partial output followed by a client-side error (connection closed / EOF) and no final usage. This is demo-impacting and has occurred twice in the last week when demos were intentionally run in eu-west for data residency narrative.

Impact:
- SE cannot reliably demonstrate streaming + retries + tool calling on eu-west.
- Workaround exists (switch demo to us-east), but undermines the EMEA/data residency talk track.
- No evidence of data loss; this is transport-level disconnect. However, it breaks the “production reliability” narrative.

Customer context:
Summit Telecom (EMEA enterprise prospect) requested eu-west demo due to data residency concerns. Disconnect happened live during the streaming portion of the talk track.

Environment:
- Demo tenant org: redwood-demo (shared)
- Region: eu-west
- Endpoint: /v1/chat/completions (stream=true)
- Transport: SSE over HTTP/2 via edge proxy
- Observed timeframe: 2025-11-12 through 2025-11-18

Symptoms:
- Client receives several SSE chunks then connection closes.
- Requests do not always show a server-side 5xx; many appear as 200 with incomplete stream.
- In gateway logs, see downstream reset / stream closed with incomplete response.

Steps to reproduce (most reliable):
1) Run demo app scenario “Structured JSON + tool call” with stream=true.
2) Add 150–250ms artificial client-side jitter (hotel Wi-Fi / VPN seems to trigger more often).
3) Use eu-west and keep the stream open >20s (longer outputs / function call explanation).
4) Observe occasional disconnect around 10–25s.

Expected:
Stream remains open until [DONE] event; client gets final chunk and usage.

Actual:
SSE stream terminates early (client EOF), forcing an awkward restart in front of customer.

Initial hypothesis:
Edge proxy / gateway idle timeout or HTTP/2 stream handling bug in eu-west path (possible region-specific config drift). Potential interaction with periodic proxy flush + buffering settings for SSE.

Immediate workaround (for Sales Engineering):
- Prefer us-east for any live streaming steps; keep eu-west for Console screenshots / narrative.
- If eu-west is required: reduce max_tokens for live step (<=256) and prefer shorter prompts; avoid long “explain reasoning” prompts.
- In the demo app, enable automatic reconnect + resume (client retries with the same conversation state) and narrate it as ‘built-in retry logic’. (This is not ideal; only for live demo continuity.)

Triage notes:
- Slack triage in #support and #sales on 2025-11-18. Markus Klein provided customer-facing symptoms; SRE pulled edge logs.
- This ticket is customer-support-style but used internally to ensure tracking, prioritization, and a concrete fix + rollout plan for the demo environment.

title: Private observability minimum pack: CI smoke tests failing (Prometheus scrape targets down / dashboards not detected)
preview:
Owner requested: SRE/Infra to triage CI instability in the Private Observability Minimum Pack smoke-test pipeline.

Issue summary
- Our GitHub Actions job "private-observability-smoke" is intermittently failing on main for redwood-private-installer + redwood-helm-charts integration.
- Failures started after the smoke-test harness changes landed and we began validating dashboard provisioning + Prometheus scrape targets as part of the gate.

Impact
- Blocks merges/releases touching the Private observability minimum pack because CI gate is red.
- Consumes SRE/Eng time due to retries; reduces confidence in pack validation.

When/where
- Pipeline: CI (GitHub Actions), kind cluster, ephemeral.
- Job: "smoke-test-private-observability" (step: post-install validation).
- First observed: 2025-08-18 evening; escalated 2025-08-19.

Symptoms (from CI output)
1) Prometheus targets never reach healthy state within timeout
- Validation expects all Redwood ServiceMonitors to be "up" within 180s.
- Error:
  - "FAIL: expected Prometheus target job=redwood-serving-runtime to be up; got state=down, lastError=context deadline exceeded"

2) Grafana dashboards provisioned but API query returns empty folder on first attempt
- Error:
  - "FAIL: expected >= 3 dashboards in folder 'Redwood Private - Minimum Pack'; got 0"

Suspected regression area
- Prometheus Operator version drift in CI base image / chart dependency.
- ServiceMonitor label selector mismatch between helm chart defaults and kube-prometheus-stack in CI.
- Race: Grafana sidecar loads dashboards async; our check hits Grafana too early.

How to reproduce locally
- On a dev machine with docker/kind:
  1. ./scripts/ci/smoke/private_observability_smoke.sh
  2. Or: make smoke-test-private-observability
- Expected: install completes, Prometheus scrapes Redwood targets, Grafana folder contains Redwood dashboards.
- Actual: intermittent failure ~40-60% across retries.

Logs (snippets)
- From CI run 2025-08-19T10:42Z:
  - "[validate] waiting for prometheus targets..."
  - "GET http://prometheus-k8s.monitoring:9090/api/v1/targets"
  - "target=serviceMonitor/redwood-private/serving-runtime state=down lastError='server returned HTTP status 403 Forbidden'"
  - "NOTE: NetworkPolicy in namespace monitoring blocks ingress from prometheus-k8s -> redwood-private"

- From CI run 2025-08-19T15:08Z:
  - "[grafana] GET /api/search?folderIds=23"
  - "HTTP 200 []"
  - "[grafana] sidecar log: 'processing 7 dashboard files' (appears ~45s after our first query)"

Attachments / references
- Slack thread: #internal-support (linked from SRE triage channel) referencing #eng-infra discussion about prom operator + selectors.
- Customer-relevant context: SUP-49210 (missing dashboards) and SUP-49238 (noisy alerts) were both triggered by packaging changes; we want CI to catch real regressions, not flake.

Request
- Infra/SRE to identify root cause and propose a stable fix (chart pin, selector alignment, timeout/backoff strategy, or policy adjustment).

title: Benchmark landing page intermittently timing out / failing to load during launch traffic (504/5xx)
preview:
Issue summary:
- Customer reported the public benchmark landing page intermittently timing out during launch-day traffic.
- Symptoms included blank page loads, long spinner, and browser console showing failed fetch for benchmark results JSON.

Customer report:
- Company: HelixCode (self-serve evaluation)
- Contact: (via support form) “Alex M., Eng”
- Reported at: 2025-07-09 10:18 PT
- Customer impact: Unable to view interactive benchmark table; blocked from sharing results internally during evaluation.

Impact (internal):
- Multiple reports from community + at least 1 prospect via support.
- Marketing-site conversion path impacted (benchmark page -> Hosted API signup / Dedicated demo CTA).

Environment:
- Production marketing site behind CDN
- Benchmark results fetched client-side from results service (JSON)

Steps to reproduce (as observed during incident window):
1) Navigate to https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar
2) Hard refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) a few times or open in an incognito window
3) Observe request to /benchmarks/results/latest.json (or similar) occasionally failing

Expected:
- Landing page renders quickly; interactive table loads benchmark dataset consistently.

Observed:
- Intermittent 504/5xx on benchmark JSON fetch; page appears to hang waiting for data.
- Some edge locations served slow responses leading to timeouts.

Attachments / evidence:
- Customer screenshot described: “Gateway timeout” on data request.
- Internal monitoring: elevated CDN 5xx and increased origin latency correlated with cache misses.

Notes:
- Linked to launch traffic spike and cache behavior; see related incident INC-2025-07-09-EDGE-03.

title: Private VPC installer fails pulling container images due to missing/incorrect ECR VPC endpoints (private DNS)
preview:
Issue summary:
Acme Bank pilot install in a private-only AWS VPC fails during the "pull images" step. Nodes cannot pull images from ECR because required VPC endpoints are missing OR private DNS is not enabled on the ECR endpoints.

Impact:
- Blocks initial Redwood Private install (hard stop during bootstrap)
- Affects pilot timeline; customer unable to proceed to validation tests

Customer / context:
- Customer: Acme Bank (regulated)
- Deployment: Redwood Private (VPC) on AWS (EKS assumed)
- Network posture: no direct internet egress from private subnets; NAT intentionally not provisioned
- Customer is using Terraform-based VPC provisioning but did not use our endpoint defaults (yet)

Environment details (as provided by customer):
- AWS Region: us-east-1
- VPC: vpc-0f2c*** (private subnets only for worker nodes)
- EKS: private cluster endpoint enabled; worker nodes in private subnets
- DNS: AmazonProvidedDNS enabled on VPC, but endpoint private DNS settings unclear
- Egress: no NAT gateway; no outbound proxy

Expected result:
Installer should pull required images (ECR) successfully when the VPC is configured with Redwood-supported endpoints/DNS.

Actual result:
Image pulls fail with DNS resolution errors / timeouts to ECR.

Steps to reproduce (customer):
1) Provision EKS cluster in private subnets (no NAT)
2) Run redwood-private-installer (v0.19.3) from a bastion inside the VPC
3) During bootstrap, installer schedules core services; nodes attempt to pull images
4) Pods remain in ImagePullBackOff / ErrImagePull

Logs / evidence:
From customer (kubectl describe pod):
- Failed to pull image "***.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/redwood/control-plane:0.19.3":
  rpc error: code = Unknown desc = failed to resolve reference ***.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/redwood/control-plane:0.19.3: failed to do request: Head "https://***.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/v2/redwood/control-plane/manifests/0.19.3": dial tcp: lookup ***.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com on 10.60.0.2:53: no such host

Additional node events:
- Warning  Failed     kubelet  Error: ErrImagePull
- Warning  Failed     kubelet  Error: ImagePullBackOff

Customer notes:
- They created "some" interface endpoints (STS, logs) but did not create ECR endpoints initially.
- After adding ECR endpoints, error changed from timeout to NXDOMAIN, suggesting private DNS not active or resolver path not reaching the endpoint.

Investigation notes (Redwood):
- For private-only subnets with no NAT, ECR requires:
  - Interface VPC endpoint: com.amazonaws.us-east-1.ecr.api
  - Interface VPC endpoint: com.amazonaws.us-east-1.ecr.dkr
  - Gateway VPC endpoint: com.amazonaws.us-east-1.s3 (for image layer download path)
  - DNS must resolve *.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com and api.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com to the endpoint ENIs (private DNS enabled on endpoints).
- Common failure mode: endpoints created but Private DNS disabled (or conflicting private hosted zone).
- Secondary common failure: endpoint security group does not allow inbound 443 from node subnets / node security group.

What we asked the customer to verify:
- Confirm these endpoints exist in the VPC and are in the same VPC as EKS nodes:
  - ecr.api, ecr.dkr, s3
- Confirm on the ECR interface endpoints:
  - "Enable Private DNS name" = true
- Confirm endpoint SG allows inbound tcp/443 from:
  - EKS worker node SG (or the node CIDR ranges)
- Confirm VPC attributes:
  - enableDnsSupport=true
  - enableDnsHostnames=true

Workaround provided (short-term):
Option A (preferred): Add/enable required endpoints
- Create interface endpoints for ecr.api and ecr.dkr with Private DNS enabled
- Ensure endpoint SG allows inbound 443 from node SG / node CIDR
- Create S3 gateway endpoint and associate to private route tables

Option B (temporary, if policy allows): Provide controlled egress
- Add NAT gateway for private subnets OR allow outbound via approved proxy to ECR/S3
- This is not recommended for the target locked-down posture; use only to unblock while endpoints are provisioned

Resolution:
Customer created the missing endpoints and reconfigured endpoint Private DNS settings:
- Added interface endpoints: ecr.api, ecr.dkr
- Enabled Private DNS on both
- Updated endpoint SG to allow 443 from the EKS node SG
- Added S3 gateway endpoint and associated it to the private route tables

After changes:
- New pods pulled images successfully
- Existing failing pods recovered after delete/recreate (or rollout restart)

Product / engineering follow-up:
- Filed as recurring pilot blocker. We should proactively detect missing endpoints during installer preflight and provide explicit remediation steps.
- Linked PR adds installer preflight detection + improved warning/error messaging:
  - sources/github/redwood-private-installer/pr-19110-detect-vpc-endpoints-and-warn-on-missing-ecr.json

Support notes / customer comms:
- Sent customer a checklist and endpoint list (ECR + S3) and asked for screenshots of endpoint Private DNS + SG rules.
- Customer confirmed resolution on 2025-06-21 during pilot check-in.

Suggested doc update (for DevEx/Docs, not blocking closure):
- Deployment guide should explicitly call out S3 gateway endpoint requirement for ECR layer pulls (in addition to ecr.api/ecr.dkr).
- Add troubleshooting section: "NXDOMAIN for *.dkr.ecr" -> check Private DNS on endpoint + conflicting private hosted zones.

Closure criteria:
- Customer install proceeds past image pull stage
- Installer preflight improvement tracked via linked PR

title: Noisy-neighbor tail latency increase for non-burst Dedicated tenant during another tenant’s burst window (us-east / H100)
preview:
Issue summary
- Northpeak Search (Dedicated, burst NOT enabled) reported elevated p99 latency and intermittent request timeouts during a 35–45 min window on 2025-05-16.
- Correlation observed between Northpeak’s latency spike and a separate tenant’s burst activity against the shared us-east H100 burst pool.

Impact
- Customer-reported: p99 latency increased from ~2.2s baseline to 6–9s for chat completion requests; a subset of requests hit client timeout (30s) and were retried.
- Redwood telemetry: elevated tail latency (p95/p99) on Northpeak’s dedicated route, plus increased queueing time at router/scheduler boundary.
- Duration: ~14:05–14:47 UTC (approx; see investigation notes).

Customer expectations / concern
- Customer is explicitly not opted into burst and expects neighbor activity not to affect their SLOs.
- Asked whether Dedicated implies strict isolation and whether Redwood is prioritizing burst traffic over reserved baseline traffic.

Environment
- prod / us-east
- Hardware class: H100
- Northpeak Dedicated baseline capacity: 8x H100 (reserved)
- Burst pool: shared managed pool, us-east H100

Initial hypotheses
1) Smart routing fallback or capacity spillover incorrectly sending burst traffic into non-burst dedicated pools.
2) Shared upstream bottleneck (router CPU, rate limiter, admission controller latency, shared KV cache service, or network) causing head-of-line blocking.
3) Fleet safety cap not engaging quickly enough; burst pool saturation caused global contention in the serving runtime / scheduler on the same cluster.
4) Telemetry lag causing the admission controller to admit too many burst requests temporarily.

What we need to answer
- Confirm whether Northpeak traffic ever ran on burst GPUs (it should not) vs. whether the impact was an upstream shared component.
- Identify the guardrail that failed (or was missing) and put a mitigation in place before continuing beta burst testing in us-east.

Customer-visible request
- Provide written explanation of root cause, assurances on isolation, and what changes were made to prevent recurrence.

Attachments / references
- Slack: #incidents thread 1739649912 (beta burst test latency event)
- Dashboards: Burst Saturation (us-east/H100), Dedicated Tenant Latency (Northpeak), Router Queue Depth (us-east)
- Runbook: Burst pool overload mitigation

title: Retention policy TTL not being applied to request/response logs in prod us-east (purge lag > configured TTL)
preview:
Issue summary:
Northbridge Bank reports that their configured retention policy for request/response logs (TTL = 7 days) does not appear to be enforced in us-east. They are seeing request log entries older than 10 days still present in the Dedicated tenant.

Impact:
- Customer is in an active enterprise security review and needs evidence that retention controls are enforced.
- Operational impact: risk of non-compliance with customer policy until backlog is cleared.
- No serving/runtime impact reported; this is a data lifecycle / purge enforcement issue.

Customer statement (paraphrased):
"We set request log retention to 7 days last week. In us-east we can still query request logs from 10–12 days ago. Is TTL actually running?"

Environment:
- Product: Dedicated
- Region: prod us-east
- Data class: request/response logs (NOT audit logs)
- Customer policy: request_logs.ttl_days=7, prompts/completions storage disabled (per customer settings)

What we expected:
- Records older than 7 days should be deleted by scheduled purge jobs with observable progress (ttl_lag_seconds trending near 0–6h depending on job schedule).

What we observed:
- ttl_lag_seconds for request_logs in us-east increased to ~280k–420k seconds (~3.2–4.9 days), suggesting purge backlog.
- purge_failures for request_logs shows spikes during/after recent telemetry DB maintenance window.

Attachments / screenshots (customer-provided):
- Screenshot 1: Console query showing request log entries dated 2025-02-27 still present (captured 2025-03-11).
- Screenshot 2: Customer internal dashboard noting retention TTL 7d set on 2025-03-04.

Internal screenshots (linked in comments):
- Grafana: Retention Controls Verification / Request Logs (us-east) showing ttl_lag_seconds elevated and purge_rows_deleted dropping to near-zero during maintenance.

Triage checklist:
1) Confirm customer policy is set and evaluated correctly (policy service + cache).
2) Confirm request logs store TTL enforcement job is enabled in us-east.
3) Check purge job backlog and throttles (safety limits / DB pressure controls).
4) Validate no policy exceptions (legal hold / incident hold) are active for this tenant.
5) If backlog exists, run safe backfill purge and monitor lag until within SLO.

Notes:
- This ticket is distinct from audit log retention/export issues (see SUP-3917). This is specifically request/response log storage TTL enforcement.

title: On-prem restore fails during manifest apply: missing CRDs / resource ordering causes "no matches for kind" errors
preview:
Issue summary
- Customer attempted a control-plane restore for Redwood Private in an air-gapped on-prem environment.
- `restore apply` fails when applying Kubernetes resources from the backup artifact.
- Errors indicate missing CRDs and/or incorrect ordering (CustomResources applied before CRDs are installed).

Impact
- Customer blocked from completing restore validation for internal DR readiness review.
- No production outage (this was a planned restore drill into a new cluster), but DR timeline at risk.

Customer context
- Customer: MedData Systems
- Deployment: Redwood Private (on-prem, air-gapped)
- Restore target: new Kubernetes cluster (fresh install)
- Backup artifact source: previous cluster (same environment) created via installer `backup create`.

What the customer expected
- Restore should bootstrap required components in a deterministic order and fail fast with clear remediation steps if prerequisites are missing.

What happened
- Restore job attempts to apply Kubernetes manifests (including CRs for cert-manager / external-secrets / redwood control-plane CRs) before the corresponding CRDs exist on the target cluster.

Steps to reproduce (as reported)
1) Stand up a fresh Kubernetes cluster (air-gapped, no external repos).
2) Install Redwood Private base prerequisites (customer believed CRDs would be included in restore bundle).
3) Run: `redwood-private restore apply --from /mnt/backup/backup_2025-02-17T0312Z`.
4) Restore fails during "Apply Kubernetes objects" stage.

Observed errors (snippets)
- kubectl apply output from restore logs:
  - "error: resource mapping not found for name: \"redwood-control-plane\" namespace: \"redwood-system\" from \"manifest.yaml\": no matches for kind \"RedwoodTenant\" in version \"platform.redwood.ai/v1\" ensure CRDs are installed first"
  - "error: unable to recognize \"manifest.yaml\": no matches for kind \"ExternalSecret\" in version \"external-secrets.io/v1beta1\""
  - "error: unable to recognize \"manifest.yaml\": no matches for kind \"Certificate\" in version \"cert-manager.io/v1\""
- Restore summary:
  - "stage=apply_k8s_objects status=failed applied=214 failed=17 skipped=0"
  - "hint: install required CRDs and re-run restore"

Notes
- The customer’s baseline cluster did not have cert-manager and external-secrets CRDs preinstalled in the restore target.
- In air-gapped mode, the customer is using the offline bundle and does not have access to pull charts/images during restore.

Request
- Provide a clear supported workaround for installing required CRDs (and in what order) prior to re-running restore.
- Confirm whether Redwood’s restore workflow should install CRDs automatically (from bundle) or explicitly require them as prerequisites.
- Update customer-facing runbook to prevent recurrence.

title: Provision dedicated GPU benchmark runner capacity for Runtime LTS perf gates (avoid blocking CI)
preview:
Request
- We need dedicated GPU runner capacity for perf-canary benchmarks used as *release gates* for Runtime LTS patch releases.
- Current state: perf jobs are contending with general CI and ad-hoc benchmark runs, causing queue times and occasional partial coverage during patch week.

Why this matters
- LTS patches are blocked on perf gate results (p50/p95 latency + throughput across canonical workloads). Any runner starvation delays releases and increases risk of releasing without full coverage.
- We also need to avoid impacting regular developer CI and nightly runs.

Scope
- Provision/allocate a separate runner pool (or reserved quota) for perf-canary “release-branch / LTS gate” workflows.
- Ensure capacity exists for concurrent runs during patch week (RC validation + reruns for flakes) without starving mainline.

Requirements (from Runtime LTS workstream)
- GPU SKUs: H100 (primary), A100 (secondary) at minimum.
- Concurrency target: 6 parallel benchmark jobs (H100) + 2 parallel (A100) during patch week; outside patch week, allow scale down to 2+1.
- Runtime: each job ~45–70 minutes wall time; expect 2–3 full gate cycles per RC (initial run + rerun(s)).
- Determinism: pin driver/container image; avoid noisy neighbors (dedicated nodes preferred).

Acceptance criteria
- New runner pool available to perf-canary with labels (e.g., runner_group=lts-perf-gate).
- perf-canary workflows updated to target that pool for release/* and runtime-*-lts branches.
- Documented scheduling policy + escalation path if capacity is exhausted.

Owners / stakeholders
- Requestor: Sahana Iyer (CI/build)
- Release Eng: Connor O’Brien
- Runtime perf: Marcus Reed / Jonas Weber
- SRE: Sean Gallagher (assignee), Rafael Mendes (backup)

References
- Slack: #eng-sre / “lts-perf-gate-thresholds” and #eng-infra / runner capacity discussion
- Related: INT-3302 for release-branch CI permissions/secrets; ENG-4825 for perf gate integration into LTS release.

title: Request: official backup/restore + DR runbook for Redwood Private (VPC) deployment (FinBank)
preview:
Issue summary:
FinBank (Private / VPC) is requesting an official, supportable backup/restore runbook for Redwood Private control plane and a DR checklist suitable for internal audit. They are in late-stage security + reliability validation and need a customer-facing guidance doc (or an approved interim runbook) to proceed.

Customer ask / urgency:
- Provide "official" Redwood guidance for: (1) consistent backups, (2) restore into a clean cluster, (3) DR procedure (single-region) with validation steps.
- Confirm what is included/excluded (Kubernetes objects vs Redwood control plane state; secrets handling).
- Provide encryption + key management expectations (customer-managed KMS key, rotation expectations, behavior if KMS unavailable).
- Provide recommended storage backend patterns (S3 + KMS + least privilege IAM).
- Provide evidence artifacts they can keep for audit (backup manifest, checksum, restore validation report).
- Timeline: they want this within 1 week to meet an internal gate for their MAP.

Environment details provided by customer:
- Deployment type: Redwood Private (VPC) on AWS EKS
- Region: us-east-1
- Topology: single-region, multi-AZ
- Object storage permitted: S3 (preferred). NFS is not preferred; GCS not applicable.

title: EU-pinned customer seeing cross-region (US) fallback attempt when EU capacity constrained
preview:
Issue summary:
Nordholm Payments reports that after enabling EU-only region pinning for their Hosted API project, they observed at least one request attempting to fall back to a US region when EU capacity was constrained. They require strict enforcement (no cross-region routing/fallback).

Impact:
- Compliance risk perception for customer (regulated payments).
- Customer paused POC traffic until confirmed fixed.

Customer expectation:
- Requests must be served only within the configured allowed regions (EU-only).
- If EU is unavailable/capacity constrained, the request must be blocked with an explicit residency violation error (not retried in US).

Environment:
- Prod
- Customer config: project pinned to EU-only; primary region: eu-west
- Observed behavior: smart router selected eu-west then attempted us-east fallback

Steps to reproduce (from customer):
1) Configure project residency policy: allowed_regions=["eu-west"], primary_region="eu-west".
2) Send chat completion request during EU capacity pressure.
3) Observe request tracing showing attempted failover to us-east.

Evidence provided:
- Request IDs (customer): rwreq_01HTNQ8Z0YB3P0K4WJ5R4K0V8S, rwreq_01HTNQ9B6K2C6X7Z7RZ0C9W3QG, rwreq_01HTNQ9H2M6ZB5K2W7VQK7X2DP
- Customer noted response header "x-redwood-region: us-east" on one request.

Notes:
- Customer asked for written confirmation of behavior and whether any prompts could have been processed outside EU.
- We should treat this as a product bug until proven otherwise (routing should never attempt cross-region fallback when policy disallows it).

title: Enable Hosted data residency enforcement feature flag in staging + seed test orgs/API keys for verification
preview:
Background
We are preparing end-to-end staging verification for Hosted data residency enforcement (region pinning + deterministic blocks + standardized errors). This internal support request is to enable the staging feature flags and seed a few test orgs/projects/API keys so Engineering + SRE + Security can validate routing, fallback behavior, logging boundaries, and the public error contract.

Request
1) Enable residency enforcement feature flags in staging
- Enable residency policy evaluation + enforcement in gateway + routing.
- Ensure cross-region fallback is disabled by default when residency constraints are present.

2) Seed staging test entities
Create the following in staging control plane (do not use real customer data):
A) Org: "residency-e2e-eu-only" (owner: platform-test@redwood.example)
- Project: "hosted-chat"
- Residency policy: allowed_regions=[EU], primary_region=EU (EU region group as defined in internal mapping)
- API key(s):
  - Key 1: project-scoped (standard)
  - Key 2: key-level override (optional) to validate precedence rules

B) Org: "residency-e2e-us-only"
- Project: "hosted-chat"
- Residency policy: allowed_regions=[US], primary_region=US
- API key: project-scoped

C) Org: "residency-e2e-unpinned"
- Project: "hosted-chat"
- No residency policy (control behavior baseline)

Notes / constraints
- Do not paste full API keys into this ticket. Store in 1Password shared vault "Staging Test Credentials" under item: "INT-9312 residency e2e keys".
- Ensure audit log emission is enabled for policy create/update/delete in staging (for evidence + UI troubleshooting).
- Ensure request logs for blocked requests include residency decision context fields (policy_id, evaluated_allowed_regions, selected_region, block_reason, request_id) without logging prompt/content.

Approvals needed
- Security Engineering sign-off for staging enablement: Marcus Lin
- Product owner sign-off for staging test plan: Naomi Feldman

Success criteria
- Staging environment can deterministically reproduce a residency block and returns the standardized error contract for both streaming and non-streaming endpoints.
- Routing does not attempt cross-region fallback when policy prohibits it.
- Console shows the seeded policies and recent blocked requests, and audit log shows config changes.

References
- Slack: #eng-sre (thread on alerting + enablement) and #eng-platform (schema/policy precedence)
- Planned enforcement/error contract PRs: PR-21507, PR-21533
- Related support bug being validated in staging: SUP-28577 (streaming returned generic 500 previously)

title: Staging: Private control-plane backup CronJob failing with RBAC "forbidden" (cannot list/get secrets + Redwood CRs)
preview:
Issue summary
- The scheduled backup CronJob deployed via Helm in staging is failing immediately due to missing RBAC permissions.
- This blocks our nightly backup verification and is causing noise for on-call.

Impact
- Staging backup schedule is effectively disabled (no successful backups since enabling the chart values).
- No customer impact (staging only), but this blocks Day-2 ops hardening and restore drill automation.

Environment
- Cluster: private-staging-us-east-1
- Namespace: redwood-private
- Chart: redwood-private (backup CronJob enabled)
- Backup storage backend: S3 (staging bucket)

Symptoms
- CronJob pods crash with "forbidden" errors when attempting to enumerate resources required for control-plane backup.

Steps to reproduce
1) Deploy/upgrade Helm release with backup enabled:
   - values.backup.enabled=true
   - values.backup.schedule="0 2 * * *"
2) Wait for CronJob to create a Job.
3) Inspect logs for the Job pod:
   kubectl -n redwood-private logs job/<job-name>

Observed logs (redacted)
- E0122 02:01:14.123] error: failed preflight: unable to read cluster state: secrets is forbidden: User "system:serviceaccount:redwood-private:redwood-backup" cannot list resource "secrets" in API group "" in the namespace "redwood-private"
- E0122 02:01:14.124] error: failed inventory: redwoodtenants.platform.redwood.ai is forbidden: User "system:serviceaccount:redwood-private:redwood-backup" cannot list resource "redwoodtenants" in API group "platform.redwood.ai" in the namespace "redwood-private"

Expected behavior
- Backup CronJob service account should have the minimal RBAC required to read backup inputs (config/state CRs, configmaps, select secrets or secret references) and create the backup artifact.

Notes
- This appears to be a Helm chart RBAC template gap (service account is created, but Role/RoleBinding is incomplete/missing required verbs/resources when backup is enabled).
